result.markdown:
# R Fundamentals and Workflow for PK/PD

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

- Set up a complete R environment for PK/PD analysis
- Understand R data structures through a pharmacometric lens
- Master the "Tidyverse" workflow for data cleaning and transformation
- Handle common PK/PD data hurdles (BLQ values, units, and pivoting)
- Build sophisticated, faceted `ggplot2` visualizations
- Organize reproducible PK/PD analysis projects

---

## Setting Up Your R Environment

### Installing R and RStudio

1. Download R from [cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org)
2. Download RStudio (or VS Code with the R extension).
3. **The PK/PD Ecosystem:** Unlike other fields, PK/PD in R relies on a specific set of high-performance packages.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Check and install core PK/PD packages if missing
core_pkgs <- c(
  "tidyverse", "PKNCA", "rxode2", "nlmixr2",
  "mrgsolve", "patchwork", "janitor", "here"
)
missing_pkgs <- core_pkgs[!core_pkgs %in% installed.packages()[, 1]]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  install.packages(missing_pkgs)
  message("Installed: ", paste(missing_pkgs, collapse = ", "))
} else {
  message("All core PK/PD packages are already installed.")
}
```
:::


### Recommended Project Structure

```
PKPD_Project/
├── data/            # Raw and processed data files
├── R/               # Custom R functions
├── scripts/         # Analysis scripts
├── output/          # Generated tables and figures
├── reports/         # Quarto/R Markdown reports
├── renv/            # Package version management (optional)
└── PKPD_Project.Rproj
```

---

## Importing PK/PD Data

### Common Data Formats in PK/PD

PK data typically has a "long" structure with columns for subject ID, time, concentration, and dose information.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate a typical PK dataset
set.seed(123)
n_subjects <- 6
times <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24)

pk_data <- expand.grid(
  ID = 1:n_subjects,
  TIME = times
) %>%
  mutate(
    DOSE = 100,
    CONC = (DOSE / 10) * exp(-0.3 * TIME) * rlnorm(n(), meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.2),
    .by = ID
  )

# View the first few rows
head(pk_data, 12)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   ID TIME DOSE      CONC
1   1 0.00  100  8.939592
2   2 0.00  100  9.147245
3   3 0.00  100 11.505858
4   4 0.00  100 10.311499
5   5 0.00  100 11.171531
6   6 0.00  100  7.988183
7   1 0.25  100  8.860025
8   2 0.25  100 11.850872
9   3 0.25  100  8.440376
10  4 0.25  100  7.388744
11  5 0.25  100  9.163267
12  6 0.25  100  8.559212
```


:::
:::


### Importing from Files


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Import CSV
pk_data <- read_csv("data/pk_concentrations.csv")

# Import Excel
library(readxl)
pk_data <- read_excel("data/pk_data.xlsx", sheet = "Concentrations")

# Import NONMEM-style data
pk_data <- read_table("data/pk_data.txt")
```
:::


### Data Cleaning and Reshaping


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Check for missing values
colSums(is.na(pk_data))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    ID   TIME   CONC   DOSE    AGE WEIGHT    SEX 
     0      0      0      0      0      0      0 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Convert columns to correct types
pk_data <- pk_data %>%
  mutate(
    ID   = as.factor(ID),
    TIME = as.numeric(TIME),
    CONC = as.numeric(CONC)
  )

# Reshape wide → long (if needed)
pk_wide <- pk_data %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = TIME, values_from = CONC,
                     names_prefix = "T_")

# Reshape long → wide
pk_long <- pk_wide %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("T_"),
                      names_to = "TIME",
                      values_to = "CONC",
                      names_prefix = "T_") %>%
  mutate(TIME = as.numeric(TIME))
```
:::


---

## Plotting Concentration-Time Profiles

### Individual Profiles


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Individual concentration-time plots
ggplot(pk_data, aes(TIME, CONC, group = ID, color = ID)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Individual Concentration-Time Profiles",
       subtitle = "IV Bolus, 100 mg dose",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
       color = "Subject") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=2400}
:::
:::


### Mean ± SD Profile


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Summary statistics and mean ± SD plot
pk_summary <- pk_data %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(CONC),
    SD   = sd(CONC),
    N    = n(),
    .by = TIME
  )

ggplot(pk_summary, aes(TIME, Mean)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Mean - SD, ymax = Mean + SD),
              fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#0B6E6B", size = 2) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Mean Concentration-Time Profile (± SD)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=2400}
:::
:::


### Semi-Log Plot (Standard PK View)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(pk_data, aes(TIME, CONC, group = ID, color = ID)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Semi-Log Concentration-Time Profiles",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)",
       color = "Subject") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=2400}
:::
:::


---

## Plotting Dose-Response Data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Simulate dose-response data
doses <- c(0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300)
n_per_dose <- 5

set.seed(456)
dr_data <- expand.grid(DOSE = doses, REP = 1:n_per_dose) %>%
  mutate(
    Emax = 100,
    EC50 = 10,
    EFFECT = (Emax * DOSE) / (EC50 + DOSE) + rnorm(n(), 0, 5)
  )

ggplot(dr_data, aes(DOSE, EFFECT)) +
  geom_point(color = "#C44536", alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Dose-Response Data",
       x = "Dose (mg, log scale)", y = "Effect (% of maximum)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=2400}
:::
:::


---

## Reproducible Scripts and Version Control

### Best Practices for PK/PD Scripts

1. **Start every script with a header** describing purpose, author, and date
2. **Load all packages at the top**
3. **Use relative paths** (not absolute paths like `C:/Users/...`)
4. **Comment your code** — explain *why*, not *what*
5. **Separate data import, analysis, and visualization**
6. **Use version control** (Git) for all scripts


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Example script header
# ============================================================
# Script: 01_nca_analysis.R
# Purpose: Noncompartmental analysis of Study PK-2024-001
# Author:  Your Name
# Date:    2024-06-01
# Packages: PKNCA, dplyr, ggplot2
# Input:   data/raw/study001_conc.csv
# Output:  output/nca_parameters.csv, output/figures/conc_time.pdf
# ============================================================
```
:::


---

## Creating a Reproducible PK/PD Workflow


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# A minimal reproducible PK analysis workflow

# 1. Load libraries
library(PKNCA)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# 2. Import data
# pk_data <- read_csv("data/pk_data.csv")

# 3. Set up NCA intervals
# my_intervals <- data.frame(start = 0, end = 24, ..., cmax = TRUE, ...)

# 4. Calculate NCA parameters
# nca_results <- pk.nca(my_data, my_intervals)

# 5. Summarize and export
# nca_summary <- summary(nca_results)
# write_csv(nca_summary, "output/nca_results.csv")

# 6. Visualize
# plot(nca_results)
```
:::


---

## Handling Missing Data in PK/PD

Missing concentration values are common in clinical PK studies. The appropriate handling strategy depends on **why** the data are missing.

### The MCAR/MAR/MNAR Framework

| Mechanism | Definition | Test/Clue | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MCAR** (Missing Completely at Random) | Missingness unrelated to any variable | No pattern in missing vs observed | Mean/median imputation OK |
| **MAR** (Missing at Random) | Missingness explained by observed covariates | Pattern linked to recorded variables (e.g., more missing at later times) | Regression imputation, multiple imputation (`mice`), or MLE-based modeling |
| **MNAR** (Missing Not at Random) | Missingness depends on the unobserved value itself (e.g., samples below LLOQ) | Plausible mechanism: high CL patients drop out, BLQ samples | Selection/pattern-mixture models + sensitivity analysis; **no method is unbiased** |


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Example: Identify and handle missing data patterns
library(dplyr)

# Simulate PK data with different missingness types
set.seed(42)
pk_missing <- data.frame(
  ID = rep(1:10, each = 8),
  TIME = rep(c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24), 10),
  CONC = 25 * exp(-0.2 * rep(c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24), 10)) * 
          exp(rnorm(80, 0, 0.12))
)

# Introduce MCAR: random 10% missing
mcar_idx <- sample(1:80, 8)
pk_missing$CONC_MCAR <- pk_missing$CONC
pk_missing$CONC_MCAR[mcar_idx] <- NA

# Introduce MAR: more missing at later time points
mar_prob <- 0.1 * pk_missing$TIME / 24
mar_idx <- which(runif(80) < mar_prob)
pk_missing$CONC_MAR <- pk_missing$CONC
pk_missing$CONC_MAR[mar_idx] <- NA

# Summarize missingness
missing_summary <- pk_missing %>%
  summarise(
    MCAR_missing = sum(is.na(CONC_MCAR)),
    MAR_missing  = sum(is.na(CONC_MAR)),
    .by = TIME
  )

cat("Missing by time point:\n")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Missing by time point:
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
print(missing_summary)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  TIME MCAR_missing MAR_missing
1  0.5            0           0
2  1.0            1           0
3  2.0            1           0
4  4.0            0           0
5  6.0            1           1
6  8.0            3           0
7 12.0            2           0
8 24.0            0           1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# Simple imputation: last observation carried forward (LOCF)
pk_missing <- pk_missing %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  tidyr::fill(CONC_MCAR, CONC_MAR, .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup()
```
:::


::: {.key-box}
### BLQ (Below Limit of Quantification) Handling

BLQ values are a special case of **MNAR** — the value is missing *because* it's low. Common approaches:

- **LLOQ/2 substitution** — set BLQ = LLOQ/2 (simple, common in NCA)
- **M3 method** — likelihood-based approach that treats BLQ as left-censored observations
- **Imputation** — multiple imputation with left-censoring constraint

For NCA, LLOQ/2 is standard. For population PK, likelihood-based methods (M3) are preferred as they correctly propagate uncertainty.
:::

---

## Beyond `rxode2`: Comparing Simulation Engines

While `rxode2` is our workhorse for ODE-based PK modeling, R offers several complementary simulation frameworks. This section introduces `mrgsolve`, `linpk`, and `nlme` — each filling a distinct niche in the PK/PD toolkit.

### `mrgsolve`: C++ Simulation for Clinical Trial Design

`mrgsolve` compiles ODE models to C++ for blazing-fast simulation. It excels at large-scale clinical trial simulations with complex dosing regimens.

Let's simulate a two-compartment IV bolus in **both** `rxode2` and `mrgsolve` to compare their syntax and output:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(rxode2)
library(mrgsolve)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# ---- Simulated patient parameters ----
params <- c(CL = 5, Vc = 50, Q = 4, Vp = 30)  # named numeric vector

# ---- rxode2 model ----
rx_model <- rxode2({
  d/dt(A1) <- - (CL/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vc) * A1 + (Q/Vp) * A2
  d/dt(A2) <-   (Q/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vp) * A2
  Cp <- A1 / Vc
})

rx_sim <- rx_model |>
  rxSolve(
    params  = params,
    events  = et(time = 0, amt = 500, cmt = 1) |> et(seq(0, 48, by = 0.5))
  ) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  dplyr::mutate(engine = "rxode2")

# ---- mrgsolve model ----
mrg_code <- "
$PARAM CL = 5, Vc = 50, Q = 4, Vp = 30
$CMT A1 A2
$ODE
dxdt_A1 = - (CL/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vc) * A1 + (Q/Vp) * A2;
dxdt_A2 =   (Q/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vp) * A2;
$TABLE
double Cp = A1 / Vc;
$CAPTURE Cp
"

mrg_model <- mcode("twocmt", mrg_code)
mrg_sim <- mrg_model |>
  ev(amt = 500, cmt = 1, time = 0) |>
  mrgsim(end = 48, delta = 0.5) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(engine = "mrgsolve")

# ---- Combine & Plot ----
bind_rows(rx_sim, mrg_sim) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = Cp, color = engine, linetype = engine)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("rxode2" = "#00A8B0", "mrgsolve" = "#E0533D")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("rxode2" = "solid", "mrgsolve" = "dashed")) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "rxode2 vs mrgsolve: 2-Compartment IV Bolus",
    subtitle = "Both engines converge to identical solutions",
    x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
    color = "Engine", linetype = "Engine"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Two-compartment IV bolus simulated with rxode2 (solid teal) and mrgsolve (dashed coral). Both engines produce identical results, confirming model equivalence.](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/fig-engine-comparison-1.png){#fig-engine-comparison width=2400}
:::
:::


| Feature | `rxode2` | `mrgsolve` |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | R-like ODE in `rxode2({...})` | C++ code block `$ODE ...` |
| **Compilation** | C (via Rcpp) | C++ (via Rcpp) |
| **Dosing** | `et()` event table | `ev()` event object |
| **Population sim** | `rxSolve()` with parameter vectors | `idata_set` with individual parameters |
| **Strengths** | Seamless nlmixr2 integration | Fastest for large-scale trial simulations |

### `linpk`: Closed-Form PK Without ODEs

For linear PK systems, you often don't need an ODE solver at all. `linpk` computes PK profiles analytically from primary parameters, making it ideal for rapid exploration.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(linpk)

# Single oral dose, 1-compartment
pk1 <- pkprofile(
  time = seq(0, 72, by = 0.25),
  dose = list(oral = data.frame(time = 0, amount = 100)),
  cl   = 5, vc = 80, ka = 0.8
)

as.data.frame(pk1) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = conc)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#00A8B0", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(color = "#00A8B0", linewidth = 1.0) +
  labs(
    title = "linpk: Closed-Form 1-CMT Oral Profile",
    subtitle = "No differential equation solver needed",
    x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Multi-dose oral profile generated with linpk using closed-form equations. No ODE solver required.](04-r-setup_files/figure-html/fig-linpk-demo-1.png){#fig-linpk-demo width=2100}
:::
:::


### `nlme`: Linear Mixed-Effects for PK Summary Statistics

Before diving into full population PK (Chapter 12), `nlme` provides a gentle introduction to mixed-effects modeling applied to PK endpoints like $C_{max}$ and AUC:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(nlme)

# Simulate peak concentrations from 3 dose groups
set.seed(456)
pk_summary <- data.frame(
  subject = rep(1:30, each = 2),
  period  = rep(1:2, 30),
  dose    = rep(c(50, 100, 200), each = 20),
  cmax    = NA
)
pk_summary$cmax <- with(pk_summary,
  2.5 * dose^0.85 * exp(rnorm(nrow(pk_summary), 0, 0.2)) +
  rnorm(nrow(pk_summary), 0, 0.5)
)

fit <- lme(log(cmax) ~ log(dose), random = ~ 1 | subject,
           data = pk_summary)
summary(fit)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Linear mixed-effects model fit by REML
  Data: pk_summary 
        AIC      BIC  logLik
  -5.953581 2.288191 6.97679

Random effects:
 Formula: ~1 | subject
        (Intercept)  Residual
StdDev:  0.02105444 0.2008386

Fixed effects:  log(cmax) ~ log(dose) 
                Value  Std.Error DF  t-value p-value
(Intercept) 1.0461150 0.21488876 30  4.86817       0
log(dose)   0.8301846 0.04631407 28 17.92511       0
 Correlation: 
          (Intr)
log(dose) -0.993

Standardized Within-Group Residuals:
        Min          Q1         Med          Q3         Max 
-2.12086963 -0.66342228 -0.01970859  0.60944753  1.95146249 

Number of Observations: 60
Number of Groups: 30 
```


:::
:::


The power model $\text{C}_{max} \propto \text{dose}^{\beta}$ estimates $\beta \approx 0.85$, confirming near-linear dose proportionality — a common regulatory question. We'll revisit `nlme` for full PK curve fitting in Chapter 12.

:::

::: {.exercise-box}
### Exercises

1. Create a simulated PK dataset with 10 subjects, 8 time points, and 2 dose groups. Plot individual and mean profiles.
2. Import a CSV file of your choice and reshape it from wide to long format.
3. Create a semi-log plot with proper axis labels and provide a caption about what the linear terminal phase indicates.
4. Write a reusable R function that calculates $k_e$ from two concentration-time points.
5. Set up a project directory structure following the recommended layout.
:::

::: {.mistakes-box}
### Common Mistakes

- **Using absolute file paths** — scripts break when shared or moved
- **Not checking for BLOQ (Below Limit of Quantification) values** — they need special handling
- **Plotting concentration on linear scale** — always check the semi-log view
- **Forgetting to convert time units** (hours → minutes, days → hours)
- **Overwriting raw data** — always keep original files unchanged
:::

::: {.key-box}
### Key Takeaway

A well-organized R environment with proper project structure, version control, and reproducible scripts is the foundation of reliable PK/PD analysis. Master data import, cleaning, and visualization in R before moving to modeling.
:::
